F.rrn Polrrrcal Pnrcrrcps CouvrrssroN
P.C. Bor S07 . +2lt J Street . Sacrlmenro. C"\ q53ll-0807
(916) l?2-5660 . Fux (9 lo) -ill-()Sljt)
October 20,2000
Jerry L. Harper, Director
Deparlment of The Youth Authority
42 4 | W illiamsb ourgh Drive
Sacramento, CA 95823-2088
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-181
Dear Mr. Harper:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").r
QIIESTION
If you become a board member of JAYCOR Company, what disciosures must you make
when you abstain from making a govemmental decision in your position as Director of the
California Youth Authority because the decision may materially affeet JAYCOR Company?
CONCLUSION
If you determine that you have a conflict of interest in a particular decision, yow
determination not to act, and disclosure of the financial interest, must be made in writing to
your appointing authority.2
FACTS
You are the Director of the California Department of the Youth Authority (CYA) and are
a designated employee. You have been invited to serve'as a member of the Board of Directors of
t Gorr"*-".rt Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 We advise only on the requirements of the Political Reform Act. We encourage you to consider whether
the doctrine of "Incompatible Activities" or Government Code section 1090 issues are triggered by your facts. The
Attorney General's office has jurisdiction over both of these areas of law.


FileNo. A-00-181
Page2
JAYCOR Company. You may receive compensation for your services on the board. At the
least, you will be reimbursed for transportation and meals related to serving as a board member
JAYCOR is a San Diego company that manufactures pepper ball dispersal units, commonly
known as pepper ball guns. These guns are used for non-lethal control of crowds and
individuals.
CYA uses pepper spray and other types of non-lethal weapons when necessary to control
offenders who are committed to CYA by the juvenile courts. You do not believe that CYA has
ever purchased goods manufactured by JAYCOR.
It is your understanding that if you accept the position with JAYCOR, you must avoid
making, participating in making, or influencing any decisions of CYA that might materially
fi nancially affect JAYC OR.
You have asked what steps you must take to cornply with the disclosure and
disqualification provisions of the Act.
AIIALYSIs
The Act's conflict of interest provisions help to insure that public offrcials perform their
duties impartially, free from bias attributable to their own financial interests or those of persons
who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public
official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
You indicate that it is your understanding that if you accept the position with JAYCOR,
you must avoid making, participating in making, or influencing any decisions of CYA that might
materially financially affect JAYCOR. For your understanding to be correct, you must have a
conflict of interest in a particular decision being made by you in your official position. You have
not provided us with enough facts to determine if you do, indeed, have a conflict with respect to
a particular decision. For your information, we briefly set forth the standard conflicts analysis to
help you determine whether your understanding is correct.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision, within the meaning
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulafion 18700(a).)
As the Director of the CYA, you are a public official,
Next, in order to trigger a conflict, you must be making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision. Those terms are described in detail in Regulations
18702.1,18702.2, and 18702.3. We enclose a copy of these regulations for your review.
Assuming that you are making, participating in making, or influencing a govemmental decision,


File No. A-00-181
Page 3
we move to the next step, identiffing "economic interests" that may trigger a conflict. There are
six kinds of economic interests described in Section 87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5.
Two of those interests may be involved in your situation. They are:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she is a
director, fficer, partner, tntstee, employee, or holds any position of management
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b));
a
a
An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
totaling $250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3).
You will be a director of JAYCOR Company. Therefore, that business entity is an
"economic interest" to you under $ 87103(d). In addition, you indicate that you will receive
reimbursement for travel and meals related to your service on the JAYCOR board, and that you
may receive compensation for your services as a board member. If these amounts total $250 or
more during a 12-month period, JAYCOR will be a "source of income" to you. ($ 87103 (c).)
The next step in analyzirrg a potential conflict of interest is to determine whether the
official's economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision(s) at
issue. (Regulation 18700(bX4).) A business entity is directly involved in a governmental
decision when it 1) initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request, or 2) is a named party in, or the subject of, the
proceeding. (Regulation 18704.1.) If the business is directly involved, then any effect of the
govemmental decision on the business is deemed to be material. (Regulation 18705.1(a).)
If the business entity is indirectly involved, Regulation 18705.1(b) contains differing
materiality standards for different types of business entities. You have not provided enough facts
about JAYCOR Company for us to determine which materiality standard will apply. However,
we enclose a copy of the regulation to assist you in making the determination. Applying these
rules, if JAYCOR is indirectly involved in a decision, you will not have a conflict in the decision
unless you determine that it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect JAYCOR as
described in Regulation 18705.1(b). As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means
"substantially likely." (Regulation 18706; Inrc Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the
financial consequences of a govemmental decision are stbstantially likely at the time the
decision is made is highly situation-specific; making this evaluation is a'Judgr4ent call." A
financial effect need not be a certainty to !e considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial
likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a
mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.)
If you determine that you have a conflict of interest in a particular decision, your next
question is what type of disclosures you are required to make under the Political Reform Act.


File No. A-00-181
Page 4
Regulation 18730(bX10) states that "[w]hen a designated employee determines that he or she
should not make a governmental decision because he or she has a disqualiffing interest in it, the
determination not to act must be accompanied by disclosure of the disqualiffing interest. ... [I]n
the case of a designated employee who is the head of an agencY, this determination and
disclosure shall be made in writing to his or her appointing authority." Regarding the contents of
the disclosure, we have advised that the following disclosure is necessary: "I disqualiff myself
because it is reasonably foreseeable that JAYCOR Company, on which I sit as a board member,
will be materially affected by the decision." (See Bennef/ Advice Letter, A-98-239.)
At its October 6,2000 meeting, the Commission tentatively decided that the disclosure
requirements of 18730(b)(10) should be permissive rather than mandatory. The Commission is
expected to approve final language at its December meeting. If this'amendment is adopted, you
would not be required to make any disclosures when you abstain from making a governmental
decision due to a potential conflict of interest in the decision.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(e16) 322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Deborah Allison

is what type of disclosures you are required to make under the Political Reform Act.

determination not to act, and disclosure of the financial interest, must be made in writing to
your appointing authority.2

QUESTION: is what type of disclosures you are required to make under the Political Reform Act.
Regulation 18730(bX10) states that "[w]hen a designated employee determines that he or she
should not make a governmental decision because he or she has a disqualiffing interest in it, the
determination not to act must be accompanied by disclosure of the disqualiffing interest. ... [I]n
the case of a designated employee who is the head of an agencY, this determination and
disclosure shall be made in writing to his or her appointing authority." Regarding the contents of
the disclosure, we have advised that the following disclosure is necessary: "I disqualiff myself
because it is reasonably foreseeable that JAYCOR Company, on which I sit as a board member,
will be materially affected by the decision." (See Bennef/ Advice Letter, A-98-239.)
At its October 6,2000 meeting, the Commission tentatively decided that the disclosure
requirements of 18730(b)(10) should be permissive rather than mandatory. The Commission is
expected to approve final language at its December meeting. If this'amendment is adopted, you
would not be required to make any disclosures when you abstain from making a governmental
decision due to a potential conflict of interest in the decision.

CONCLUSION: If you determine that you have a conflict of interest in a particular decision, yow
determination not to act, and disclosure of the financial interest, must be made in writing to
your appointing authority.2
